title: Emergency Guide: Dog Swallowed a Button Battery | Vet Triage
meta_desc: If your dog swallowed a button battery, this is a life-threatening emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is required to prevent severe burns.
content_html:
TL;DR: Button battery ingestion is a life-threatening medical emergency that can cause severe internal burns within two hours. Seek immediate veterinary care and bring a photo of the battery packaging to help determine the best treatment.

What is button battery ingestion and why is it dangerous for my pet?

Button batteries are small, coin-shaped discs often found in hearing aids, remote controls, and car fobs. While they appear harmless, they are extremely dangerous if swallowed. Unlike standard batteries, these can cause catastrophic internal damage in as little as two hours due to their chemical makeup and electrical charge.

Is my pet swallowing a button battery considered a medical emergency?

  • Yes, this is a critical medical emergency with a high urgency level.
  • When a button battery gets stuck in the esophagus or enters the stomach, it creates an electrical circuit with the moist tissue.
  • This leads to severe internal burns (liquefaction necrosis), tissue death, and potential perforation of the digestive tract.
  • Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately; do not wait for symptoms to appear.

How does taking a photo of the battery packaging help my veterinarian during triage?

  • Take a clear photo of the original packaging or the device the battery came from if possible.
  • Providing the specific model number (such as CR2032) helps the vet understand the battery's diameter, thickness, and voltage.
  • This information is vital for determining the best course of action, whether it be emergency surgery or endoscopic removal.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following button battery ingestion in dogs, radiography is recommended to determine battery location, but some batteries may not be visible. If the battery has not passed through the pylorus within 48 hours or if there is evidence of puncture, surgical or endoscopic removal may be necessary to prevent gastric or intestinal ulceration due to leakage of alkaline gel. Emesis is contraindicated if the battery casing may be punctured due to the risk of alkaline gel exposure to the oral and esophageal mucosa. If the battery is intact, induction of emesis may result in expulsion. Boluses of 20 mL of tap water every 15 minutes will decrease the severity and delay esophageal ulceration. Serial radiographs to verify battery location are recommended until the battery is expelled.

Chapter: Toxicology, Gastroenterology, Surgery

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3003)

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting if my dog swallowed a battery?
faqs.answer: No. Never induce vomiting for a battery ingestion unless specifically instructed by a veterinarian. If the battery has been damaged or is leaking, bringing it back up can cause severe chemical burns to the esophagus.

faqs.question: What are the early warning signs of internal damage?
faqs.answer: Early symptoms may include excessive drooling, difficulty swallowing, pawing at the mouth, abdominal pain, or a sudden refusal to eat.

faqs.question: Can a button battery pass through a dog naturally?
faqs.answer: While a battery may occasionally pass, the risk of it becoming lodged or leaking corrosive material is extremely high. Professional imaging (X-rays) and veterinary monitoring are mandatory to ensure the battery does not cause fatal internal injuries.
